title: Multi-Agent Systems
description: Supervisor patterns, specialist agents, planner-executor designs, communication, debugging, and anti-patterns.

questions.title: When should you use a multi-agent system?
questions.scenario: A team proposes five agents for a simple FAQ assistant.
questions.expectedAnswer: Use multi-agent systems when tasks benefit from specialization, parallel work, independent critique, or separate responsibilities. Avoid them when a single workflow or model call is enough. Multi-agent designs add coordination overhead, latency, cost, debugging difficulty, and emergent failure modes.
commonMistakes: Answering with definitions only and not connecting to a practical system.
Ignoring tradeoffs such as cost, latency, quality, safety, and maintainability.
Failing to mention how the approach would be evaluated in production.
followUpQuestions: How would you validate this for Multi-Agent Systems?
What would change at enterprise scale?
What failure modes would you monitor?
questions.realWorldExample: In an enterprise multi-agent systems setting, the team should connect the answer to user workflow, governance, observability, and measurable business impact.
questions.interviewerNotes: A strong candidate should give a structured answer, make assumptions explicit, discuss tradeoffs, and explain how they would test the decision rather than relying on buzzwords.

questions.title: Explain the supervisor agent pattern.
questions.scenario: A research assistant needs search, summarization, fact-checking, and report-writing specialists.
questions.expectedAnswer: A supervisor agent routes tasks to specialist agents, monitors progress, aggregates results, and decides when work is complete. It centralizes coordination but can become a bottleneck or single point of failure. Strong answers include routing criteria, state management, guardrails, and tracing.
commonMistakes: Answering with definitions only and not connecting to a practical system.
Ignoring tradeoffs such as cost, latency, quality, safety, and maintainability.
Failing to mention how the approach would be evaluated in production.
followUpQuestions: How would you validate this for Multi-Agent Systems?
What would change at enterprise scale?
What failure modes would you monitor?
questions.realWorldExample: In an enterprise multi-agent systems setting, the team should connect the answer to user workflow, governance, observability, and measurable business impact.
questions.interviewerNotes: A strong candidate should give a structured answer, make assumptions explicit, discuss tradeoffs, and explain how they would test the decision rather than relying on buzzwords.

questions.title: What is the planner-executor pattern?
questions.scenario: An agent must gather information, compare options, and produce a recommendation.
questions.expectedAnswer: The planner decomposes a task into steps while executor agents or tools perform each step. This separates strategy from action and improves controllability. Risks include bad plans, stale plans, excessive steps, and poor feedback between execution and replanning.
commonMistakes: Answering with definitions only and not connecting to a practical system.
Ignoring tradeoffs such as cost, latency, quality, safety, and maintainability.
Failing to mention how the approach would be evaluated in production.
followUpQuestions: How would you validate this for Multi-Agent Systems?
What would change at enterprise scale?
What failure modes would you monitor?

questions.interviewerNotes: A strong candidate should give a structured answer, make assumptions explicit, discuss tradeoffs, and explain how they would test the decision rather than relying on buzzwords.

questions.title: How should agents communicate?
questions.scenario: Two specialist agents keep debating and never produce a final answer.
questions.expectedAnswer: Agents should communicate through structured messages with task state, assumptions, outputs, confidence, and required next actions. Avoid unbounded chat loops. Use schemas, shared memory, event logs, and explicit termination criteria to make communication testable.
commonMistakes: Answering with definitions only and not connecting to a practical system.
Ignoring tradeoffs such as cost, latency, quality, safety, and maintainability.
Failing to mention how the approach would be evaluated in production.

questions.title: How do you debug multi-agent systems?
questions.scenario: A multi-agent workflow is expensive and occasionally returns contradictory conclusions.
questions.expectedAnswer: Debugging requires traces of messages, tool calls, state changes, decisions, costs, and outputs. Replayable runs, deterministic seeds where possible, scenario tests, per-agent metrics, and failure taxonomies help identify routing errors, loops, tool misuse, or context loss.
commonMistakes: Answering with definitions only and not connecting to a practical system.
Ignoring tradeoffs such as cost, latency, quality, safety, and maintainability.
Failing to mention how the approach would be evaluated in production.

questions.title: How would you explain specialist agent design in a Multi-Agent Systems interview?
questions.scenario: An interviewer asks you to reason through specialist agent design for an enterprise AI use case where correctness, cost, security, and adoption all matter.
questions.expectedAnswer: A strong answer should define specialist agent design, explain why it matters for multi-agent systems, and connect it to a practical implementation decision. The candidate should describe the core mechanism, identify where it appears in a production system, discuss tradeoffs such as quality, cost, latency, safety, governance, and maintainability, and name how they would evaluate whether the approach is working. For senior or architect-level interviews, the answer should also include failure modes, ownership boundaries, and rollout considerations.
